nativeRange.habitat: Turbulent, fast-flowing whitewater rapids of the lower Rio Xingu in the vicinity of Belo Monte (Pará state, northern Brazil); shelters among rocky substrates and water-worn boulders in well-oxygenated channels. Wild habitat is impacted by the Belo Monte dam complex.
nativeRange.biotope: Rio Xingu rocky rapids (lower Xingu, near Belo Monte)

decorPreferences: numerous tight-fitting caves (ceramic pleco caves, clay tubes, slate stacks) — at least one per individual
water-worn boulders and rocky structures forming crevices
sand or fine smooth gravel substrate
driftwood for supplementary cover and biofilm
strong directional flow (powerheads, large filter returns) with high dissolved oxygen
dim or shaded lighting to encourage daytime activity

diet.notes: The King Tiger Pleco leans toward meat rather than plant matter, setting it apart from many other pleco species that graze algae heavily. Juveniles tend to be more broadly omnivorous, while adults generally prefer meaty foods such as bloodworms, chopped mussel, prawn, mysis shrimp, and high-protein sinking pellets. Vegetable matter (blanched zucchini, cucumber, or courgette) is accepted sparingly but should not form the dietary staple. Feeding after lights-out is generally advised, as this species is strongly nocturnal.

goodWith: Apistogramma and other small dwarf cichlids (with adequate caves)
other small non-aggressive bottom dwellers that won't compete for caves
warm-water dwarf shrimp (with adequate cover) as juveniles, though adults may opportunistically prey on shrimplets
avoidWith: other Hypancistrus species (hybridisation risk — L066 readily crosses with L046 Zebra, L333, L260 Queen Arabesque, etc.)
fast, boisterous tankmates that monopolise food
fish large enough to harass or predate on plecos
other cave-claiming plecos that will dispute territory

summary: The King Tiger Pleco (L066) is a Rio Xingu Hypancistrus recognised by its intricate maze of black-and-cream scribble markings. Carnivore-leaning and strongly cave-dependent, it is regularly captive-bred and is generally regarded as one of the more accessible Hypancistrus for intermediate keepers.
careNotes:
The King Tiger Pleco shares the fast-moving whitewater rapids of the Rio Xingu with the L046 Zebra Pleco and is generally regarded as one of the more accessible members of the Hypancistrus genus for intermediate keepers, available through specialty retailers at considerably lower prices than the Zebra Pleco. It is generally advised to keep this species in warm (around 26–29°C), well-oxygenated, soft to moderately hard water with strong directional flow that simulates its native whitewater rapids. Filtration should turn over the tank volume roughly 4–6× per hour at minimum, with supplemental powerheads recommended along the substrate.

The L-numbers associated with this species can be confusing in the trade. L066, L333, L399, and L400 are all scribble-patterned Hypancistrus from the lower Xingu that have historically been traded under separate names — L333 was generally said to carry a finer pale marking pattern, while L400 was described as having a squatter body. In 2025 these were formally synonymised under the newly described species Hypancistrus seideli, confirming what hobbyists had long suspected: that they represent a single highly variable species. In practice many tank-raised specimens are mixed-lineage, and all of these L-numbers cross-breed freely with one another and with the L046 Zebra Pleco. For this reason it is strongly advised not to house this species alongside other Hypancistrus if breeding is a consideration.

Tight-fitting caves are mandatory — each fish should have at least one cave snug enough that the body just fits, as territory is centred on a cave. Commercial ceramic pleco caves, clay flowerpot tubes, or carefully stacked slate all work well. Providing more caves than fish helps reduce disputes between individuals. Water quality must be kept high; this species has been reported to be intolerant of accumulated organic pollutants, and weekly water changes of 30–50% are commonly recommended.

Unlike most plecos, the King Tiger Pleco is a poor algae grazer. Its diet should centre on meaty foods such as bloodworms, mysis, chopped mussel or prawn, and high-protein sinking pellets, with only occasional vegetable supplementation. Feed primarily at or after lights-out, as the species is strongly nocturnal.

The vast majority of trade specimens today are captive-bred, which is fortunate given that wild Rio Xingu populations have been heavily impacted by the Belo Monte dam complex. Captive-bred fish are generally hardier and accept prepared foods more readily, but still benefit from warm, high-flow, well-oxygenated conditions matching the wild biotope.
breedingNotes:
L066 is a cave-spawner with paternal care, and is regularly bred in captivity — considered one of the more accessible Hypancistrus to breed once water conditions are dialled in. Males defend a tight-fitting cave and entice receptive females inside, where spawning takes place. Clutch size is small (commonly reported around 5–15 adhesive eggs). The male guards and fans the eggs for approximately 5–10 days until hatching; fry remain in the cave absorbing the yolk sac for a further week or so before becoming free-swimming.

Conditioning breeders on a varied high-protein diet (bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis, quality carnivore pellets) and performing slightly cooler, larger water changes (simulating the seasonal Xingu flood pulse) are commonly cited spawning triggers. Around 28–29°C with slightly acidic pH (6.5–7.0) is generally recommended for breeding. Fry are typically raised on newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, micro-worm, and finely crushed high-protein pellets, with the same high-flow, well-oxygenated conditions as the adult tank. Hybridisation risk with other Hypancistrus (L046, L333, L260) is a real concern — keep breeders strictly species-only.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hypancistrus sp. L066
primary.notes: Used as primary source after PlanetCatfish (originally designated primary) returned HTTP 403. Provided native range (lower Rio Xingu, Belo Monte vicinity), water parameters (26–30°C, pH 5.0–7.0, hardness 36–268 ppm), adult size (12–14 cm SL), tank footprint (120×45 cm / ~243 L), diet (meaty foods preferred over vegetables), nocturnal habit, cave-spawning behaviour, relations to L333 and L400, and habitat-threat note from Belo Monte dam construction. Note: SeriouslyFish text extraction listed family as 'Doradidae' which is incorrect; family is Loricariidae per FishBase/Wikipedia and corrected accordingly.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hypancistrus
additional.notes: Used to confirm taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes, Ancistrini), correcting the SeriouslyFish 'Doradidae' extraction error. Also confirmed common-name aliases (King Tiger Pleco, Scribbled Pleco, Network Pleco) and that L066 and L333 are commonly treated together in the trade.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — King Tiger Pleco (L333 Hypancistrus sp.)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (11.4–12.7 cm, exceptionally to ~16 cm), lifespan (10–15 years), temperature (25–28°C), pH (5.5–7.5), tank size (~150 L minimum), nocturnal behaviour, peaceful temperament, omnivorous-leaning-protein diet, and cave-spawning breeding biology. Confirms L066/L333 are commonly used interchangeably and readily cross-breed.

additional.name: River Park Aquatics — L066 King Tiger Pleco
additional.notes: UK trade source. Cross-checked adult size (10–12 cm), lifespan (10–15 years), temperature (24–28°C), pH (6.5–7.5), nocturnal habit, peaceful-but-territorial-conspecific temperament, omnivorous diet with carnivore lean.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Hypancistrus sp. L066 (originally designated primary; HTTP 403)
additional.notes: Designated primary source per slice manifest but returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on the species-page URL (both /common/species.php and /catelog/species.php variants). Primary role swapped to SeriouslyFish, which was the designated secondary. Logged here for traceability so a human reviewer can revisit when the site is reachable.

fish.breedingNotes: The King Tiger Pleco is a cave-spawner with paternal care. The male defends a tight-fitting cave and entices a receptive female inside to spawn, after which he fans and guards the clutch — commonly reported at around 5–15 adhesive eggs — for roughly 5–10 days until hatching. Fry remain inside the cave absorbing their yolk sac for approximately another week before becoming free-swimming. The species is considered one of the more accessible Hypancistrus to breed once water conditions are established; success is commonly associated with warm (28–29°C), slightly acidic, high-flow water and slightly cooler, larger water changes to simulate the seasonal Xingu flood pulse. A strict species-only breeding setup is strongly advised, as this species readily hybridises with other Hypancistrus including L046, L333, and L260.
